title: Paying Attention to Facts: Quantifying the Knowledge Capacity of Attention Layers
abstract: In this paper, we investigate the ability of single-layer attention-only transformers (i.e. attention layers) to memorize facts contained in databases from a linear-algebraic perspective. We associate with each database a 3-tensor, propose the rank of this tensor as a measure of the size of the database, and provide bounds on the rank in terms of properties of the database. We also define a 3-tensor corresponding to an attention layer, and empirically demonstrate the relationship between its rank and database rank on a dataset of toy models and random databases. By highlighting the roles played by the value-output and query-key weights, and the effects of argmax and softmax on rank, our results shed light on the ‘additive motif’ of factual recall in transformers, while also suggesting a way of increasing layer capacity without increasing the number of parameters.

paragraphs: Large language models (LLMs) based on transformers with attention [10] have proven successful at a large variety of tasks. While much research has focused on how well LLMs accomplish tasks, less has been done on how LLMs accomplish these tasks, and on the nature of the tasks themselves. In this paper, we take a step towards a better theoretical understanding of how a single attention-only layer can memorize facts, such as those contained in databases, RDF triple stores or knowledge graphs.

While our work is inspired by recent papers on factual recall that have sought to uncover the underlying mechanisms behind it [3, 8, 12, 5] or to derive scaling laws for knowledge capacity [7, 2], it differs from prior research in 3 key ways: 1) Unlike prior research which includes multi-layer perceptron (MLP) layers [5, 8], we focus solely on the role of attention layers. 2) We propose linear algebraic measures of database and attention layer size, in contrast to prior results on scaling laws which are phrased in terms of number of triples and number of model parameters [7, 1]. 3) Instead of probing trained LLMs and reverse-engineering weights to determine where and how facts are stored [3, 8], we start from a theoretical perspective and build towards a mechanistic understanding of knowledge storage.

Our motivation for these choices are the observations in the literature that transformers, especially attention-only models, have ‘an enormous amount of linear structure’ [4], and that the task of factual recall in transformers is solved additively [3], a phenomena termed the ‘additive motif’. These observations suggest that a linear algebraic approach to the question of factual recall in attention layers should yield interesting results. One of the key invariants in linear algebra is the rank of a linear operator, but discussions about model rank in the literature are limited to mentions of model dimensions (e.g. the embedding dimensions and attention-head dimensions) and usually for the purpose of computing other measures of model size such as the number of parameters (e.g. [7, 2]). In-depth investigations into the properties of the rank of models or databases are conspicuously missing.

The objective of this paper is to address these gaps, and provide an alternative linear-algebraic angle to this active area of research. Post-hoc interpretability research can sometimes feel like trying to look for a needle in a haystack, and we hope this work makes the task slightly easier by helping to reveal what the needle looks like. This paper represents a small step in the direction of defining theoretically-grounded and empirically-supported measures of database and model size, and in highlighting the different roles played by the query-key and value-output matrices in factual recall. We hope that this will shed additional light on the additive motif, as well as on the difficulties in interpreting models, even simple ones. As we shall see, even in our highly simplified setting, the role of the rank of databases and of attention layers is clouded by many complications and far from being completely resolved. Nevertheless, we hope our work inspires future research in similar directions on developing a theoretical understanding of tasks and how language models accomplish them.

subsections.title: 1.1 Outline
paragraphs: The paper is organized as follows: in Section 2, we associate with each database a 33-tensor, propose the rank of this tensor as a linear-algebraic measure of the size of the database, and derive bounds for its rank in terms of database properties. In Section 3, we similarly construct a 33-tensor associated to an attention layer and provide bounds on its rank. Our results suggest a method of increasing layer capacity without increasing the number of parameters. We also investigate the effect of argmax\mathrm{argmax} and softmax\mathrm{softmax} on rank, and propose methods of assessing model accuracy that are less sensitive to these effects. We reinforce our theoretical results with illustrative examples, as well as with experiments conducted on a dataset of toy databases and models in Section 4. Finally, in Section 5, we summarize our findings and discuss limitations and extensions of our work.

We will be interested in databases, by which we mean collections of tables or dataframes like those in Fig. 1. To each such database, we can associate a “language” consisting of Resource Description Framework (RDF) triples or “sentences” of the form “[subject] [predicate] [object]”. We are interested in encoding or storing the information from a database in single-layer attention-only transformer models. By ‘encoding or storing’, we mean training a model on RDF triples from the database, so that the model can correctly complete partial triples of the form “[subject] [predicate]”. For example, a model trained on the sentences in Fig. 1 should predict “Singapore” as the next token, when given the incomplete sentence “Astrid born_in”.

This memorization task is a simplification of how larger transformer models store facts of the form, “France’s capital is Paris”.111An LLM trained on natural language would have to handle the contenation of separate tokens like ‘capital’ and ‘is’ into the predicate ‘capital is’, but in this paper, we avoid such issues and simply assume that all subjects, predicates and objects are their own single tokens. We wish to understand which parts of a transformer are capable of storing such information and how the information is encoded in the weights of the model.
Abstractly, a database is a partial function

where 𝒯\mathcal{T} is the set of all tokens in 𝒟\mathcal{D}, and ↛\nrightarrow denotes a function that is only defined on a subset of its domain. Let 𝒦,𝒬\mathcal{K},\mathcal{Q} and 𝒱\mathcal{V} denote the subsets of 𝒯\mathcal{T} consisting of tokens that appear in DD as subjects, predicates, and objects, respectively.222Subjects, predicates and objects can be thought of as keys, queries and values, resp., hence the letters 𝒦,𝒬\mathcal{K},\mathcal{Q} and 𝒱\mathcal{V}. Note that 𝒦,𝒬\mathcal{K},\mathcal{Q} and 𝒱\mathcal{V} need not be disjoint. We can then treat 𝒟\mathcal{D} as a partial function 𝒦×𝒬↛𝒱\mathcal{K}\times\mathcal{Q}\nrightarrow\mathcal{V}.

We will also use 𝒟\mathcal{D} to refer to the set of tuples {(k,q,v)|𝒟​(k,q)=v}\{(k,q,v)|\mathcal{D}(k,q)=v\}. In addition statements of the form “(k,q,v)∈𝒟(k,q,v)\in\mathcal{D}”, we will say “(k,q)∈𝒟(k,q)\in\mathcal{D}” if (k,q)(k,q) is in the domain of the function 𝒟\mathcal{D}, i.e. (k,q,v)∈𝒟(k,q,v)\in\mathcal{D} for some vv.
To any such database, we may associate a 3-tensor D∈ℝ|𝒦|×|𝒬|×|𝒱|D\in\mathbb{R}^{|\mathcal{K}|\times|\mathcal{Q}|\times|\mathcal{V}|} where

Slices of DD are matrices Dk::∈ℝ|𝒬|×|𝒱|,D:q⁣:∈ℝ|𝒦|×|𝒱|,D_{k::}\in\mathbb{R}^{|\mathcal{Q}|\times|\mathcal{V}|},D_{:q:}\in\mathbb{R}^{|\mathcal{K}|\times|\mathcal{V}|}, and D:⁣:v∈ℝ|𝒦|×|𝒬|,D_{::v}\in\mathbb{R}^{|\mathcal{K}|\times|\mathcal{Q}|}, obtained by fixing k,qk,q or vv and varying the other dimensions, while fibers of DD are the vectors Dk​q:D_{kq:}, Dk:vD_{k:v} and D:q​vD_{:qv} obtained by fixing a pair of k,qk,q or vv.

We define the rank of a database to be the rank of its associated tensor.333The rank of a 33-tensor is the minimum number of simple tensors 𝐮⊗𝐯⊗𝐰\mathbf{u}\otimes\mathbf{v}\otimes\mathbf{w} that need to be summed up to compose it. Unlike the rank of a matrix (i.e. 2-tensors), which can easily be determined (e.g., via Gaussian elimination), there are no easy ways to determine the rank of an nn-tensor when n>2n>2; indeed, even approximating tensor rank is NP-hard [6, 9]. The number of triples in a database, which we denote |𝒟||\mathcal{D}|, gives an upper bound to its rank. But we can get a tighter bound:

subsections.title: 2.1 Low-rank representations of databases
paragraphs: Before moving on to the rank of attention layers, we pause to point out a peculiarity in how factual recall is usually evaluated. A standard practice when assessing the factual recall of models is to train models on all triples (k,q,v)(k,q,v) in a database (e.g. the ‘sentences’ at the bottom of Figure 1), and evaluate them by passing only the initial pairs (k,q)(k,q) into the model and comparing the output logits against vv. For example, if a model has been trained on the database in Figure 1, we would evaluate it by getting it to complete sentences of the form ‘Astrid born_in’, and checking if the predicted token is ‘Singapore’.

Consider what this means in terms of DD: rather than evaluating the model’s predictions on the whole of DD, we are only evaluating against the fibers Dk​q:D_{kq:} for (k,q)∈𝒟(k,q)\in\mathcal{D}. We do not get the model’s predictions on ‘nonsensical’ sentences of the form ‘Malaysia born_in’, although we should if we wish to evaluate the model’s ability to reproduce the whole of DD. We ought to check that the model predicts none of the tokens to a high degree of certainty i.e. that Dk​q:D_{kq:} is close to 0 for (k,q)∉𝒟(k,q)\notin\mathcal{D}. But this is rarely done, for the obvious practical reason that there are simply too many ‘nonsensical’ sentences to evaluate the model on.

In this paper, we will continue to adopt the conventional practice of evaluating only on (k,q)∈𝒟(k,q)\in\mathcal{D}. But we keep in mind that this could significantly reduce the rank required to memorize a database, as the following example shows:
Consider a database with the following ‘facts’:
The tensor associated to this database has a single vv-slice given by the following matrix with rank 55:

However, this database is very easy to ‘memorize’: simply return vv regardless of which pairs of input are provided. This corresponds to setting D:⁣:vD_{::v} to be the all-ones matrix, which has rank 1.

Given a database 𝒟\mathcal{D} and tensor DD as defined in (1), say that another tensor EE represents 𝒟\mathcal{D} as long as Ek​q:=Dk​q:E_{kq:}=D_{kq:} for (k,q)∈𝒟(k,q)\in\mathcal{D}. We may define the effective rank of 𝒟\mathcal{D} to be the lowest rank of tensors EE that represent DD. As the preceding example shows, the effective rank of 𝒟\mathcal{D} (1 in the example) can be much less than the actual rank of 𝒟\mathcal{D} (5 in the example).

We do not have good theoretical bounds for the effective rank of 𝒟\mathcal{D}, and we will not mention effective rank in the rest of the paper. We simply note what this digression tells us: that models trained on databases are actually seeking to reproduce low rank representations of DD, rather than DD itself. As a consequence, we will expect the bounds in Proposition 2.1 to significantly over-estimate the model rank required to memorize a database.

Although we do not go further into this issue, it is clear that it has implications for LLM hallucinations when fed nonsensical inputs (e.g. ‘Where is Malaysia born?’). One way to address this issue is to expand our model evaluation methods to include random selections of nonsensical inputs, and perhaps also including special tokens to allow the model to indicate that it does not know the answer to such questions.

We now turn to the toy transformer models that we will be using: single-layer attention-only decoders, or simply attention layers. Following [4, 8], our models will be simplified versions of the original transformer architecture [10] without layer normalization, biases or positional encodings.

An attention layer ℒ\mathcal{L} is determined by the following parameters and weights: where dh​e​a​d,q​k,dh​e​a​d,v​o≤dm​o​d​e​l≤|𝒯|d_{head,qk},d_{head,vo}\leq d_{model}\leq|\mathcal{T}|. We will not assume that dh​e​a​d,q​kd_{head,qk} and dh​e​a​d,v​od_{head,vo} are equal. As we shall see, model capacity depends more on dh​e​a​d,v​od_{head,vo} than on dh​e​a​d,q​kd_{head,qk}.
• |𝒯||\mathcal{T}|, the number of tokens (commonly denoted dv​o​c​a​bd_{vocab})
• nh​e​a​d​sn_{heads}, the number of attention heads
• dm​o​d​e​ld_{model}, the embedding dimension
• dh​e​a​d,q​kd_{head,qk}, the dimension of the query-key matrices
• dh​e​a​d,o​vd_{head,ov}, the dimension of the value-output matrices
• WE∈ℝ|𝒯|×dm​o​d​e​lW_{E}\in\mathbb{R}^{|\mathcal{T}|\times d_{model}} and WU∈ℝdm​o​d​e​l×|𝒯|W_{U}\in\mathbb{R}^{d_{model}\times|\mathcal{T}|}, the embedding and unembedding matrices

• WQh∈ℝdm​o​d​e​l×dh​e​a​d,q​kW^{h}_{Q}\in\mathbb{R}^{d_{model}\times d_{head,qk}} and WKh∈ℝdh​e​a​d,q​k×dm​o​d​e​lW^{h}_{K}\in\mathbb{R}^{d_{head,qk}\times d_{model}}, the query and key matrices for each head h≤nh​e​a​d​sh\leq n_{heads}

• WVh∈ℝdm​o​d​e​l×dh​e​a​d,v​oW^{h}_{V}\in\mathbb{R}^{d_{model}\times d_{head,vo}} and WOh∈ℝdh​e​a​d,v​o×dm​o​d​e​lW^{h}_{O}\in\mathbb{R}^{d_{head,vo}\times d_{model}}, the value and output matrices for each head h≤nh​e​a​d​sh\leq n_{heads}
Instead of considering these matrices separately, we form the following |𝒯|×|𝒯||\mathcal{T}|\times|\mathcal{T}| matrices (called circuits in [4]) whose ranks are bounded by their inner dimensions:
Given a sequence of tokens 𝐱=(x1,x2,…,xn)∈𝒯n\mathbf{x}=(x_{1},x_{2},\dots,x_{n})\in\mathcal{T}^{n}, we may associate a matrix X∈ℝn×|𝒯|X\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times|\mathcal{T}|},
where 𝐞xi\mathbf{e}_{x_{i}} has 11 in the xit​hx_{i}^{th} position and 0 everywhere else. Given XX, the model produces an n×|𝒯|n\times|\mathcal{T}| matrix of logits:
where \polyline(0,1)(0,0)(1,0)(0,1) is auto-regressively masked row-wise softmax, given by:

We can then either apply row-wise argmax to get the next token argmax​(Z​(𝐱)n:)\mathrm{argmax}(Z(\mathbf{x})_{n:}), or apply (non-masked) row-wise softmax to get a probability distribution softmax​(Z​(𝐱)n:)\mathrm{softmax}(Z(\mathbf{x})_{n:}) over the next token.

subsections.title: 3.1 The rank of an attention layer
paragraphs: We wish to compute the rank of an attention layer so that we can compare it to the rank of a database. Our hypothesis is that an attention layer can only fully memorize or encode the data in a database if the layer rank exceeds the database rank. We will see that this hypothesis is only partly true.

So far, our discussion about attention layers applies to the general setting where the length of input “sentences” (x1,…,xn)(x_{1},\dots,x_{n}) may vary from sentence to sentence. From now on, we will specialize to sentences of length at most 33.

In this situation, we may define two 3-tensors per attention head. The first tensor Vh∈ℝ|𝒦∪𝒬|×|𝒬|×|𝒱|V^{h}\in\mathbb{R}^{|\mathcal{K}\cup\mathcal{Q}|\times|\mathcal{Q}|\times|\mathcal{V}|} represents the value-output circuit, and has entries
VhV^{h} can be visualized as a loaf of sliced bread, with each qq-slice equal to the same |𝒦∪𝒬|×|𝒱||\mathcal{K}\cup\mathcal{Q}|\times|\mathcal{V}| submatrix of WV​OhW^{h}_{VO}.
The second tensor Ah∈ℝ|𝒦|×|𝒬|×|𝒦∪𝒬|A^{h}\in\mathbb{R}^{|\mathcal{K}|\times|\mathcal{Q}|\times|\mathcal{K}\cup\mathcal{Q}|} is derived from the query-key or attention circuit, and has entries

AhA^{h} contains the values of the attention matrix for all possible pairs (k,q)∈𝒟(k,q)\in\mathcal{D}. We have (Ak​q​kh,Ak​q​qh)=softmax((WQ​Kh)q​k,(WQ​Kh)q​q))(A^{h}_{kqk},A^{h}_{kqq})=\mathrm{softmax}((W^{h}_{QK})_{qk},(W^{h}_{QK})_{qq})), which is the last row of \polyline(0,1)(0,0)(1,0)(0,1) ​(X​WQ​Kh​X⊤)\begin{picture}(1.0,1.0)\polyline(0,1)(0,0)(1,0)(0,1) \end{picture}(XW^{h}_{QK}X^{\top}) when 𝐱=(k,q)\mathbf{x}=(k,q).

Multiplying AhA^{h} with VhV^{h}, we get a |𝒦|×|𝒬|×|𝒱||\mathcal{K}|\times|\mathcal{Q}|\times|\mathcal{V}| tensor whose qt​hq^{th} slice is the matrix product A:q⁣:h​V:q⁣:hA^{h}_{:q:}V^{h}_{:q:}, with entries
Finally, we define the tensor E∈ℝ|𝒦|×|𝒬|×|𝒱|E\in\mathbb{R}^{|\mathcal{K}|\times|\mathcal{Q}|\times|\mathcal{V}|} representing the embed-unembed circuit:
This is another loaf with each kk-slice equal to the same |𝒬|×|𝒱||\mathcal{Q}|\times|\mathcal{V}| submatrix of WE​UW_{EU}.
Putting these together, we get a tensor
and by construction of LL, we have the following:
The fibers Lk​q:L_{kq:} are the logits when the sequence (k,q)(k,q) is fed into the model:
i.e. the final logits of ZZ from (2) evaluated on 𝐱=(k,q)\mathbf{x}=(k,q).

This proposition and the construction of LL show that WE​UW_{EU} (manifested in EE) and WV​OhW^{h}_{VO} (manifested in VhV^{h}) act as linear operations when computing the output logits. By contrast, WQ​KhW^{h}_{QK} acts more like a look-up table for populating AhA^{h}. The construction of LL, and specifically of AhA^{h} and VhV^{h} and their composition, makes explicit the observation in [4] that ‘logits are a linear function of tokens’. It also sheds light on the ‘additive motif’ of [3] by showing how the values in WV​OhW^{h}_{VO} add up to approximate DD. We illustrate with an example:
We show how the circuits in an attention layer might be defined so as to give the slices D:β⁣:D_{:\beta:} and D:λ⁣:D_{:\lambda:} from Example 2.2. Consider a single head with the following circuits:
We have V:β⁣:1=V:λ⁣:1=WV​O1V^{1}_{:\beta:}=V^{1}_{:\lambda:}=W^{1}_{VO}, while the slices of A1A^{1} are
The slices of the product A1​V1A^{1}V^{1} are:

Applying argmax\mathrm{argmax} to these slices gives the slices D:β⁣:D_{:\beta:} and D:λ⁣:D_{:\lambda:}. We have not needed the WE​UW_{EU} circuit here, but an actual model is likely to offload some values to this circuit.

This example shows that a single value-output circuit of rank 22 can account for both slices D:β⁣:D_{:\beta:} and D:λ⁣:D_{:\lambda:} even though they have a combined rank of 33. This is partly achieved by exploiting the shared values of β\beta and λ\lambda on bb and cc (as alluded to in Example 2.2), but also from the fact that rank​(A1​V1)=3\mathrm{rank}(A^{1}V^{1})=3 even though rank​(V1)=2\mathrm{rank}(V^{1})=2. Indeed, rank​(A​B)≰min⁡(rank​(A),rank​(B))\mathrm{rank}(AB)\not\leq\min(\mathrm{rank}(A),\mathrm{rank}(B)) for 33-tensors.

Note also that rank​(A1)=4\mathrm{rank}(A^{1})=4 even though rank​(WQ​K1)=1\mathrm{rank}(W^{1}_{QK})=1, showing that the rank of WQ​K1W^{1}_{QK} can be low without compromising the rank of A1A^{1}. (In fact, we would have the same A1A^{1} even if all the entries of WQ​K1W^{1}_{QK} were zero.)

On one hand, this example shows that direct encoding of facts is possible at the level of circuits, and suggests that interpreting layers might be easier at the circuit level, rather than at the level of weights or embeddings. On the other hand, even though the weights in this example were chosen to be simple, we are already seeing that WV​O1W^{1}_{VO} need not look at all like either D:β⁣:D_{:\beta:} or D:λ⁣:D_{:\lambda:}. If rank​(L)\mathrm{rank}(L) exceeds rank​(D)\mathrm{rank}(D), there will be infinitely many ways for the circuits to be defined to give DD, and it is reasonable to suppose that many of these circuits will be very hard to interpret, with the values of one slice of DD spread over multiple heads and over the embedding circuit as well.

We define the rank of a model ℒ\mathcal{L} to be the rank of LL from (8). Based on the preceding discussion and example, we expect the rank of a model to depend more on the ranks of WE​UW_{EU} and WV​OhW^{h}_{VO}, but not so much on WQ​KhW^{h}_{QK}. We have the following upper bound for the rank of LL as defined in (8):

Along each qq-slice, we have rank​((Ah​Vh):q⁣:)≤rank​(V:q⁣:h)≤rank​(WV​Oh)≤dh​e​a​d,v​o\mathrm{rank}((A^{h}V^{h})_{:q:})\leq\mathrm{rank}(V^{h}_{:q:})\leq\mathrm{rank}(W^{h}_{VO})\leq d_{head,vo}. As there are |𝒬||\mathcal{Q}| such slices, we have rank​(Ah​Vh)≤dh​e​a​d,v​o⋅|𝒬|\mathrm{rank}(A^{h}V_{h})\leq d_{head,vo}\cdot|\mathcal{Q}|. Meanwhile, rank​(E)≤rank​(WE​U)≤dm​o​d​e​l\mathrm{rank}(E)\leq\mathrm{rank}(W_{EU})\leq d_{model}. Finally, applying the subadditivity of rank yields the result. ∎

Surprisingly, the inequality contains |𝒬||\mathcal{Q}|, which is a property of 𝒟\mathcal{D} rather than ℒ\mathcal{L}, suggesting that the capacity of an attention layer might depend on the context in which it is being trained and evaluated. To get a tighter upper bound, we should replace |𝒬||\mathcal{Q}| with the number of qqs that each head contributes to, which could vary from head to head. On the other hand, to get a lower bound, we feel it is reasonable to assume that each head contributes to at least one qq. We also feel it is reasonable to assume that the process of training an attention layer will cause the spans of EE and VhV^{h} to have as little intersection as possible, so that we are likely to have

Note that dh​e​a​d,q​kd_{head,qk} does not appear in Proposition 3.5 or (9). As the example shows, rank​(Ah)\mathrm{rank}(A^{h}) can be much higher than rank​(WQ​Kh)\mathrm{rank}(W^{h}_{QK}), so dh​e​a​d,q​kd_{head,qk} does not significantly affect the capacity of the attention layer. Indeed, the WV​OhW^{h}_{VO} weights bear the burden of storing the entries of DD, while the WQ​KhW^{h}_{QK} weights are only responsible for indicating which key-query pairs in 𝒟\mathcal{D} are relevant for each head.

Now that we have defined the rank of a database and the rank of an attention layer, we might guess that a test for whether an attention layer can memorize a database would be rank​(D)≤rank​(L).\mathrm{rank}(D)\leq\mathrm{rank}(L). Combining Proposition 2.1 and (9) gives the following condition:

While this is a sufficient condition, it assumes that LL exactly replicates DD i.e. L=DL=D, which is overly strict. Indeed, we usually either take argmax​(Lk​q:)\mathrm{argmax}(L_{kq:}) if we want to get a token prediction, or softmax​(Lk​q:)\mathrm{softmax}(L_{kq:}) if we want to get a probability distribution over output tokens. A more appropriate condition would be rank​(D)≤rank​(argmax​(L))\mathrm{rank}(D)\leq\mathrm{rank}(\mathrm{argmax}(L)) or rank​(D)≤rank​(softmax​(L))\mathrm{rank}(D)\leq\mathrm{rank}(\mathrm{softmax}(L)). One would hope that rank​(argmax​(L))\mathrm{rank}(\mathrm{argmax}(L)) and rank​(softmax​(L))\mathrm{rank}(\mathrm{softmax}(L)) are related to rank​(L)\mathrm{rank}(L) in some way. Unfortunately, as we shall see, argmax\mathrm{argmax} and softmax\mathrm{softmax} can depend very little on rank​(L)\mathrm{rank}(L).

subsections.title: 3.2 Effects of argmax and softmax on rank
paragraphs: Recall the definitions of row-wise argmax\mathrm{argmax} and softmax\mathrm{softmax}:
If rank​(M)=1\mathrm{rank}(M)=1, it is easy to see that rank​(argmax​(M))≤2\mathrm{rank}(\mathrm{argmax}(M))\leq 2. But if rank​(M)≥2\mathrm{rank}(M)\geq 2, anything goes:
For 2≤r≤n2\leq r\leq n, there are n×nn\times n matrices MM with rank​(M)=r\mathrm{rank}(M)=r but rank​(argmax​(M))=n.\mathrm{rank}(\mathrm{argmax}(M))=n.

Take nn distinct points 𝐯1,…,𝐯n\mathbf{v}_{1},\dots,\mathbf{v}_{n} on the unit sphere in ℝr\mathbb{R}^{r}, and let MM be their Gram matrix: Mi​j=⟨𝐯i,𝐯j⟩M_{ij}=\langle\mathbf{v}_{i},\mathbf{v}_{j}\rangle. Then MM is a rank rr matrix with ones along the diagonal and off-diagonal entries strictly less than 11, so argmax​(M)\mathrm{argmax}(M) is the n×nn\times n identity matrix. ∎
With an extra step, we can get a similar result for softmax\mathrm{softmax}:
For 2≤r≤n2\leq r\leq n, there are n×nn\times n matrices MM with rank​(M)=r\mathrm{rank}(M)=r but rank​(softmax​(M))=n.\mathrm{rank}(\mathrm{softmax}(M))=n.

Let MM be the matrix from the previous proof. Simply choose a large enough c>0c>0 such that (eMi​i)c>∑j≠i(eMi​j)c(e^{M_{ii}})^{c}>\sum_{j\neq i}(e^{M_{ij}})^{c} for all ii. This is always possible as long as the diagonal is strictly greater than the off-diagonal entries, as its exponent will eventually grow large enough to dominate the other entries. Then rank​(c​M)=rank​(M)=r\mathrm{rank}(cM)=\mathrm{rank}(M)=r, but softmax​(c​M)\mathrm{softmax}(cM) is diagonally dominant, hence invertible. ∎

Both of these results are somewhat artificial, as they assume that we can compute with arbitrarily small or arbitrarily large magnitudes. Having limited machine precision would reduce the maximum possible rank of argmax​(M)\mathrm{argmax}(M), as it would prevent us from distinguishing points that are too close. Similarly, the proof for softmax\mathrm{softmax} requires that we can compute with large enough cc. It seems unlikely that we will encounter such extreme examples when dealing with logits produced by attention layers. Indeed, the fact that a ‘softmax bottleneck’ has been observed (e.g. in [11]) suggests that softmax does not significantly increase the rank of matrices encountered in real-world scenarios. Nevertheless, these results act as a warning that argmax\mathrm{argmax} and softmax\mathrm{softmax} can potentially distort rank.
In order to regain some control on rank, we will define a modified version of softmax. Beyond rank considerations, this modification is motivated by a separate consideration:

Suppose we have a vector of next-token probabilities 𝐩=(p1,…,p|𝒯|)\mathbf{p}=(p_{1},\dots,p_{|\mathcal{T}|}). Such a vector might be produced by applying softmax\mathrm{softmax} to the logits Lk​q:L_{kq:}, for example. If the correct token is tt, ptp_{t} represents the probability that we get the correct token if we draw from the distribution 𝐩\mathbf{p}, which might still be very small even if it is larger than all the other probabilities. It would be too lenient to say that a prediction is correct as long as ptp_{t} is the largest.

A better way would be to additionally require that ptp_{t} exceed a certain threshold τ\tau, e.g. τ=0.5\tau=0.5 or 0.90.9. If τ≥0.5\tau\geq 0.5, the condition that pt≥τp_{t}\geq\tau automatically implies that ptp_{t} is the largest in 𝐩\mathbf{p}. With this in mind, for τ∈[0.5,1]\tau\in[0.5,1], we define
We also define the τ\tau-accuracy to be

This function simply counts the number of correct predictions, divided by the total number of triples in 𝒟\mathcal{D}. We say that a layer τ\tau-memorizes a database if acc≥τ​(L,D)=1\mathrm{acc}_{\geq\tau}(L,D)=1. In this case, we have D=softmax≥τ​(L)D=\mathrm{softmax}_{\geq\tau}(L). So we expect that a model ℒ\mathcal{L} can τ\tau-memorize 𝒟\mathcal{D} if

One would hope that rank​(softmax≥τ​(L))\mathrm{rank}(\mathrm{softmax}_{\geq\tau}(L)) now bears some relation to rank​(L)\mathrm{rank}(L). Unfortunately, by choosing large enough cc in the proof of Proposition 3.7, we can theoretically also get rank​(softmax≥τ)\mathrm{rank}(\mathrm{softmax}_{\geq\tau}) to take any value.444For example, the cc for Proposition 3.7 also works for τ=0.5\tau=0.5. Nevertheless, we can hope that choosing a large enough τ\tau will cause the cc required to be unreasonably big, so that in practice softmax≥τ\mathrm{softmax}_{\geq\tau} will not increase the rank by too much.

We will see empirically that varying τ\tau allows us to control how far the rank of softmax≥τ​(L)\mathrm{softmax}_{\geq\tau}(L) deviates from the rank of LL. As τ\tau increases, rank​(softmax≥τ​(L))\mathrm{rank}(\mathrm{softmax}_{\geq\tau}(L)) decreases and gets closer to rank​(L)\mathrm{rank}(L), so that (10) becomes a good indicator of whether ℒ\mathcal{L} can memorize 𝒟\mathcal{D}. However, when τ\tau is too big, rank​(softmax≥τ​(L))\mathrm{rank}(\mathrm{softmax}_{\geq\tau}(L)) can become less than rank​(L)\mathrm{rank}(L), and eventually goes to 0 when τ=1\tau=1 (since a softmax probability of 11 requires the other logits to be −∞-\infty).

We have so far focused on defining and understanding the theoretical properties of the ranks of databases and attention layers. We turn now to experimental results to shed further light on these quantities. We make use of a small dataset of randomly generated databases and attention layers trained on these databases. Our dataset consists of 548 databases with at most 200 triples, and 364 attention layers with 1≤dh​e​a​d,o​v,dh​e​a​d,k​q≤dm​o​d​e​l≤61\leq d_{head,ov},d_{head,kq}\leq d_{model}\leq 6 and 1≤nh​e​a​d​s≤41\leq n_{heads}\leq 4.

We begin by showing the relationship of the rank bounds in Proposition 2.1 and (9) to other popular measures of database size and attention layer size. Figure 2(a) shows that the number of database triples varies approximately linearly with the upper bound of database rank from Proposition 2.1, with the number of triples being approximately 1.8 times the database rank upper bound. This suggests that both rank​(𝒟)\mathrm{rank}(\mathcal{D}) and |𝒟||\mathcal{D}| can be used as measures of database size, after the appropriate scaling factor has been accounted for.

On the other hand, Figure 2(b) shows that the number of (non-embedding) parameters and the lower bound from (9) are still positively correlated, but non-linearly and with wider spread, as is to be expected by comparing (9) with

From this dataset, 3,947 random pairs of databases and attention layers were selected, and the layers were trained on database triples for a maximum of 2,000 epochs. The final (training) accuracy at various thresholds was recorded.555We are interested in evaluating how well attention layers have memorized the training set, so there is no separate test set. We evaluate accuracy based only on final token predictions, but the model does not ‘know’ this, and seeks to predict earlier tokens too. Figure 3(a) shows how well these attention layers have succeeded in memorizing the databases, assuming that predictions are taken using argmax of logits. We see that even tiny models of rank up to 10 can memorize databases of rank 80, providing evidence of the significant rank-increasing effect of argmax\mathrm{argmax} that we might expect based on Proposition 3.6.

Figures 3(b) to 3(d) show how the memorization success changes when we use softmax≥τ\mathrm{softmax}_{\geq\tau} with varying values of τ\tau. When τ=0.5\tau=0.5, the results were almost identical to Figure 3(a) (and are thus omitted), showing that softmax≥0.5\mathrm{softmax}_{\geq 0.5} can distort rank as much as argmax\mathrm{argmax}. When τ=0.75\tau=0.75 and 0.950.95, we see a stronger dependence between layer rank and database rank. At τ=0.75\tau=0.75, a layer can memorize a database whose rank is about 3-4 times the layer rank, while at τ=0.95\tau=0.95, this factor drops to 1-2. However, when τ=0.99\tau=0.99, attention layers cannot even memorize databases with rank less than rank​(L)\mathrm{rank}(L), suggesting that τ=0.99\tau=0.99 is too strict of a requirement (or more epochs are needed).

Across all values of τ\tau, increasing the lower bound from (9) increases the memorization accuracy, showing that despite its failure to account for the effects of softmax\mathrm{softmax}, the bound is still a meaningful measure of attention layer capacity.

Finally, Figure 4 shows the effect of varying dh​e​a​d,v​od_{head,vo} and dh​e​a​d,q​kd_{head,qk} on accuracy with softmax≥0.95\mathrm{softmax}_{\geq 0.95}, and corroborates our observation following (9) that dh​e​a​d,v​od_{head,vo} ought to play a bigger role than dh​e​a​d,q​kd_{head,qk} in determining the capacity of an attention layer. We see that while increasing dh​e​a​d,v​od_{head,vo} and dh​e​a​d,q​kd_{head,qk} both generally increase the average accuracy, the effect is more pronounced with dh​e​a​d,v​od_{head,vo}. Indeed, accuracy increases from top right to bottom left along all diagonals of constant dh​e​a​d,v​o+dh​e​a​d,q​kd_{head,vo}+d_{head,qk}, suggesting that we can increase capacity without more parameters by raising dh​e​a​d,v​od_{head,vo} at the expense of dh​e​a​d,q​kd_{head,qk}.

subsections.title: 5.1 Contributions
paragraphs: In this paper, we have attempted to understand fact storage in attention layers from a linear-algebraic perspective. We have introduced novel 33-tensors DD and LL corresponding to databases and attention layers, and proved bounds for their rank, while also providing empirical evidence of the usefulness of these quantities as well as the impact of argmax\mathrm{argmax} and softmax\mathrm{softmax} on rank.

Our main theoretical contribution is the construction of LL, which provides a deeper understanding of the roles of the query-key and value-output weights, and (9), which provides a measure of the capacity of an attention layer. By decoupling dh​e​a​d,v​od_{head,vo} from dh​e​a​d,q​kd_{head,qk}, we showed that we can increase model capacity without increasing model size by increasing dh​e​a​d,v​od_{head,vo} at the expense of dh​e​a​d,q​kd_{head,qk}, a result that we have observed empirically. We have shown by example that it is possible to use the components of LL to guide the encoding of facts directly into circuits, but also that even with hand-crafted examples, it remains hard to localize where specific facts are stored.

subsections.title: 5.2 Limitations and Extensions
paragraphs: Our paper suggests many extensions and directions for future work. Although we focus on the case of database triples, it is not hard to generalize DD to an nn-tensor storing nn-gram statistics, while LL will still have the same form as (8), but with more involved AhA^{h} and VhV^{h}. Of course, our rank bounds will not hold in this more general setting, so new bounds will have to be derived. Our remarks in Section 2.1 concerning low-rank representations of DD still hold, and future work could look into deriving better bounds on the effective rank of DD. General efforts to study the effect of softmax\mathrm{softmax} on rank in more realistic settings, and to derive tighter bounds on the tensor rank will be also useful.

We expect the addition of layer norms and biases to only minimally affect the definition of the rank of an attention layer, but the effect of positional embeddings, MLP layers and having multiple layers is unknown. Even in our original setting, we note in Figure 4 that for small values of dh​e​a​d,v​od_{head,vo}, increasing dh​e​a​d,q​kd_{head,qk} causes an increases in accuracy that is not accounted for by (9), suggesting that more investigation needs to be done to derive better bounds on rank​(L)\mathrm{rank}(L) and rank​(Ah)\mathrm{rank}(A^{h}) that involve dh​e​a​d,q​kd_{head,qk}. Note also, however, that when dh​e​a​d,v​od_{head,vo} is larger, the positive effect of increasing dh​e​a​d,q​kd_{head,qk} starts to vanish and even starts to become detrimental. More work is needed to confirm if this pattern persists in larger models, and why.

On the experimental front, we have only investigated small toy models and databases. It remains to be seen if our results continue to hold for larger models trained on larger databases. We anticipate that the rank-increasing effects of softmax\mathrm{softmax} will become more severe as the rank of LL increases, and it might be necessary to scale τ\tau according to the rank of LL, instead of keeping it constant across models as we have done. As the models and databases scale up, it will also quickly become infeasible to construct and study the tensors DD and LL holistically. Future work might thus need to consider ways of analysing sub-tensors of DD and LL and their rank.

In addition to considering much larger models and databases, future work could make the databases more realistic by adding noise and missing data, by using real-world databases or RDF triple stores, and by expressing facts in natural language instead of formal RDF triples. The impact of varying dh​e​a​d,v​od_{head,vo} and dh​e​a​d,q​kd_{head,qk} separately could also be tested on language models that are not solely trained for database memorization, to see if reducing dh​e​a​d,q​kd_{head,qk} has detrimental effects on other tasks.

Evidently, our work is just a small step, and suggests more questions than it answers. Nevertheless, we hope it demonstrates the usefulness and potential of a tensor-theoretic treatment of tasks and models, and motivates further work in this direction for other tasks and types of models.
